reply_text:
@buihuy5211 So, the solution is divorce? This is why I say godless people should stay away from this sacred institution. Marriage is a solemn covenant, not a mere human contract that can be abandoned at will. When a man and a woman take their vows, they promise to love and support each other "for better or for worse, in sickness and in health, till death do us part." These words are not meant to be spoken lightly but to serve as a reminder of the deep commitment that marriage requires.

To say that waiting for a man to change is futile is to disregard the sacred duty of perseverance in marriage. It is true that a husband must fulfill his role with love, respect, and responsibility. However, if he falters, does this mean his wife should immediately lose faith in the marriage? And what about the woman? If she fails, should the man divorce his wife? The vow does not promise commitment only in good times but also requires enduring trials with patience and wisdom.

This does not mean that a woman should accept mistreatment or remain in a situation that endangers her well-being. However, it does mean that marriage is not meant to be discarded simply because a spouse is slow to change. Families are not broken because waiting for change is impossible; they are broken because of selfishness—when patience, love, and the sanctity of marriage are abandoned in favor of immediate satisfaction. If a man must grow, let him be encouraged to do so. If he must learn to be a better husband, let him be guided with love rather than dismissed as incapable of change.

Marriage is not built on convenience. You may not have time to sustain a family, but others do. Even if my wife were to receive the presidential medal for narcissism, misandry, and childishness, she would still be my beloved wife. Only sexual adultery and death will separate us—and even in the case of adultery, may God help me navigate such a situation.

Nevertheless, the show, based on the trailer, provides us with an important reminder: "Prevention is better than cure." We live in an age where feelings override reason. The protagonist’s mistake was rushing into marriage straight out of high school. Most marriages today lack courtship or premarital counseling as a foundation. Simply put, you reap what you sow—sow sparingly, and you will reap sparingly.
